title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - All Critical Infrastructure
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: UK and EU impose first joint cyber sanctions, blaming Russia’s FSB Center 16 for the attack on Poland’s grid
items.content_text: On 13 July the UK and EU formally attributed December 2025’s attempted sabotage of Poland’s electricity grid to Russia’s FSB Center 16 and imposed their first joint package of cyber sanctions, hitting more than 30 individuals and entities. Polish authorities say the operation reached over 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturer and a combined-heat-and-power plant, and attempted to deploy destructive wiper malware that could have cut heat and power to roughly half a million people in midwinter. It failed. The same unit — also tracked as Berserk Bear, Energetic Bear and Dragonfly — was named that day in a 20-nation advisory, AA26-194A, for a decade of quietly compromising internet-exposed routers across energy, water and telecoms. For OT defenders the pairing is the point: the edge gear bridging IT and the plant is a confirmed target, not a theoretical one.

items.title: SonicWall SMA1000 zero-days exploited — a CVSS 10 hole in the remote-access box many OT sites depend on
items.content_text: SonicWall is warning that attackers exploited two previously unknown flaws in its SMA1000 secure-access appliances before patches existed. CVE-2026-15409 is a CVSS 10.0 server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface that an unauthenticated remote attacker can use to force the appliance to make requests to unintended destinations; it is being chained with CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2), a post-authentication command-injection flaw in the Appliance Management Console. CISA added both to the KEV catalog on 14 July and set a 17 July deadline for federal agencies under BOD 26-04 — patch or pull the product. Affected models include the 6210, 7210 and 8200v. SMA appliances frequently broker vendor and engineer access into plant networks, which makes an unauthenticated foothold here an IT/OT boundary problem, not just an IT one.

items.title: Attackers keep exploiting a critical PTC Windchill flaw — a manufacturing PLM foothold toward the plant floor
items.content_text: Threat actors are actively exploiting a critical remote-code-execution flaw in PTC Windchill, the product-lifecycle-management platform that anchors engineering and manufacturing data across automotive, aerospace, defense and heavy-industry suppliers. CVE-2026-12569 is an improper-input-validation issue that a remote, unauthenticated attacker can trigger with crafted requests. CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog — the first PTC product ever listed — after PTC confirmed attackers were planting persistent JSP webshells for remote command execution and data theft. Because Windchill holds the design files, bills of materials and process documentation that feed OT and production, a compromised server is both a supply-chain intelligence goldmine and a staging point toward the plant floor. Patches and mitigations began shipping 17 June; internet-exposed instances still need triage.

items.title: Lantronix EDS5000 serial servers exploited in the wild — root on the box that sits between operators and the process
items.content_text: CISA added CVE-2025-67038, a critical command-injection flaw in Lantronix EDS5000 serial-to-IP device servers, to the KEV catalog on 23 June with a three-day federal remediation deadline. An unauthenticated attacker can inject operating-system commands through the username parameter and execute them as root. Forescout, which disclosed the flaw as part of its BRIDGE:BREAK research, observed a threat actor tagged Chaya_006 hitting honeypots with it as early as 5 April — after Lantronix had patched but before public disclosure. These converters bridge legacy serial equipment onto Ethernet in utility, industrial, transportation and healthcare networks, so root on one means an attacker can tamper with the data operators use to make decisions, or pivot deeper. Firmware 2.2.0.0R1 or later has been available since 20 February.

items.title: Cal Water’s investigation found no OT activity after Iran-linked Handala claimed it could disrupt the water supply
items.content_text: California Water Service has closed out its investigation into the intrusion claimed by Handala, an Iran-linked group that presents itself as hacktivist but is widely assessed to front for Iranian state operations. Handala leaked roughly 5 GB of data and asserted it could have disrupted the water supply but chose not to — a claim implying deep ICS access. Mandiant, brought in to investigate, found no evidence of threat-actor activity in Cal Water’s internal IT or OT environments; the access was limited to a small number of user accounts on two third-party service-provider platforms, including one customer’s online account. This is a useful calibration point: adversary claims of OT reach routinely outrun the forensics, and the gap between “claimed” and “confirmed” is where panic and bad decisions live.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: California Water Service / Mandiant — via SecurityWeek

items.title: AA26-194A: twenty nations detail how FSB Center 16 quietly owns routers — and how to shut it down
items.content_text: The joint advisory published alongside the UK/EU sanctions is the operational half of the story. NSA, CISA, the FBI and DC3 — with the UK NCSC, France’s ANSSI, Canada’s Cyber Centre, Australia’s ASD, New Zealand’s NCSC and other allied agencies — describe FSB Center 16 actors scanning broad IP ranges for internet-facing devices running SNMPv1 or SNMPv2 that still accept default or common community strings, then using that access to persist on routers across communications, defense industrial base, energy, financial services, government (especially state and local) and healthcare networks. The recommended mitigations are unglamorous and effective: move to SNMPv3 with authPriv and modern encryption, disable Cisco Smart Install everywhere, and patch CVE-2018-0171. For OT programmes, the finding worth escalating is that the router layer is treated as infrastructure nobody owns.

items.title: Armored Likho targets electric power operators — with a loader that looks like an LLM wrote it
items.content_text: Kaspersky has detailed a newly named APT it tracks as Armored Likho, running cyber-espionage against government and electric power organizations in Russia, Brazil and Kazakhstan alongside financially motivated attacks on individuals. Initial access is spear-phishing: archives carrying executables or LNK files that show a decoy document while a loader pulls a Python interpreter and payload archive from GitHub repositories in the background. The payload is BusySnake Stealer, a Python infostealer that decrypts its own bytecode only at call time, harvests browser passwords, cookies, OTP keys and Telegram sessions, and stands up a reverse SSH tunnel for persistent interactive access. Kaspersky assesses the group’s activity overlaps with Eagle Werewolf. Researchers flagged loader samples containing verbose inline comments and redundant code blocks — stylistic tells consistent with LLM-generated code. For OT programmes the relevance is not the malware but the harvest: stolen engineer credentials and live SSH tunnels into a power operator’s IT estate are exactly how an adversary reaches the OT boundary without ever writing an exploit.

items.title: July ICS Patch Tuesday: a CVSS 10 in Siemens Opencenter X, plus critical Logix and 1715 flaws from Rockwell
items.content_text: Siemens, Schneider Electric and Rockwell Automation shipped July’s ICS Patch Tuesday advisories on 14–15 July, fixing dozens of flaws. Siemens published nine advisories, six of them critical: the headline is a CVSS 10.0 token-invalidation flaw in Opencenter X allowing authentication bypass and full application access, with further critical issues in Mendix, Sidis Secured SmartPlug, SIMATIC S7-1500, Cadra and Desigo CC. Rockwell published twelve advisories including two critical — an unauthenticated path to intrusive CLI commands on 1715 Redundant IO (read or delete files, stop tasks, change I/O states, modify memory), and three denial-of-service holes in CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers that can force a major non-recoverable fault. Schneider addressed high-severity flaws in IGSS and EcoStruxure Cybersecurity Admin Expert. VDE CERT added five advisories covering Murrelektronik, Mettler Toledo, Codesys and Wago.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: Siemens ProductCERT, Rockwell PSIRT, Schneider Electric, CISA, VDE CERT — via SecurityWeek

items.title: CISA’s 14 July batch: ABB Ability Edgenius privilege escalation and an unauthenticated debug port on Rockwell’s 1715-AENTR
items.content_text: CISA published four ICS advisories on 14 July. ICSA-26-195-04 covers the Rockwell Automation 1715-AENTR EtherNet/IP Adapter at versions 3.003 and earlier, which exposes a network-accessible debug port that fails to enforce privilege controls — giving unauthenticated remote callers access to intrusive CLI commands. ICSA-26-195-02 addresses ABB Ability Edgenius and CVE-2026-31431, a Linux kernel weakness that lets a locally authenticated user or a compromised container workload escalate to root on affected systems. ICSA-26-195-01 concerns ABB Advant Master Online Builder, where an incorrect version of Online Builder shipped on the installation media. Edgenius sits at the industrial edge aggregating plant data, so a container-to-root escape there is a meaningful boundary failure rather than a local-only nuisance.

items.title: CISA’s 9 July advisories hit Schneider protection relays, PowerChute shutdown software and OpenPLC
items.content_text: CISA issued three ICS advisories on 9 July with a distinctly energy and water flavour. Two cover Schneider Electric: the Easergy MiCOM Px40 series — protection relays deployed widely in substations and industrial power distribution — and PowerChute Serial Shutdown, the software that coordinates graceful shutdown of equipment on UPS power. The third covers OpenPLC v3, the open-source PLC runtime, flagged for both water and wastewater systems and energy. Protection relays deserve particular attention because they sit directly in the safety and reliability path: a relay that misoperates, or fails to operate, has physical consequences that no IT-side compensating control addresses. OpenPLC’s presence is a reminder that open-source runtimes turn up in production and lab environments more often than asset inventories admit.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: CISA ICS Advisories — Schneider Electric and OpenPLC

items.title: NERC CIP-012-2 took effect 1 July, tightening protection of control-center communications
items.content_text: NERC’s revised reliability standard CIP-012-2 became enforceable on 1 July 2026, sharpening requirements to protect real-time assessment and monitoring data exchanged between bulk-electric-system control centers. It applies to Balancing Authorities, Reliability Coordinators, Transmission Operators and Owners, and Generator Operators and Owners. Requirement R1 obliges responsible entities to document plans mitigating unauthorized disclosure and modification of that data in transit, loss of the ability to communicate between control centers, and the means to initiate recovery of those links — adding explicit confidentiality and availability protections over its predecessor. For North American electric entities the effective date is a hard milestone: those protections must be implemented and evidenced, not merely planned. The underlying principle travels well beyond NERC’s footprint — the links between control centers are critical assets in their own right.

items.title: EPA’s National Cyber Drill pulled the internet out from under water utilities — and 66 of 67 never proved they could go manual
items.content_text: The EPA’s 2026 National Cybersecurity Drill dropped participating drinking-water and wastewater utilities into a scenario built on Salt Typhoon reporting: a cyberattack degrades a tier-one telecommunications provider, and utilities must run for three days with no SCADA remote connectivity to outstations, no cloud services, no email and no VoIP. More than 200 utilities and 390 participants joined. The EPA also offered a live-action module in which utilities would actually attempt the transition to manual operations rather than talk through it; 67 opted in, and just one confirmed it had carried the transition out. That single data point is the finding. Most utilities have a documented manual-operations procedure and have never executed it against a clock, which means the assumption underwriting every “we can always run it by hand” risk acceptance is, for practical purposes, untested. It also lines up with CISA’s CI Fortify push for operators able to run safely while isolated for weeks to months.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: US EPA 2026 National Cyber Drill — via GovInfoSecurity / ISMG

items.title: White House launches “Gold Eagle,” an AI-assisted clearinghouse meant to triage vulnerabilities across critical infrastructure
items.content_text: On 14 July the White House stood up GOLD EAGLE, a vulnerability coordination clearinghouse mandated by the June executive order on AI innovation and security. Run with Treasury, DHS/CISA and the Department of War, and built on Carnegie Mellon SEI’s existing VINCE coordination platform, it is intended to pool vulnerability findings from government, open-source projects and critical infrastructure operators, apply frontier AI models to rank what actually matters, and push prioritised remediation guidance back out to defenders. The stated goals are cutting duplicated scanning and shortening the gap between disclosure and patch. For OT asset owners the promise is appealing and the caveat is familiar: prioritisation output is only useful if it understands that a medium-severity flaw on a safety-instrumented system outranks a critical on a print server. Nothing announced changes anyone’s current patch obligations.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: The White House — via Cybersecurity Dive & SecurityWeek
